title: Hello!
text: I’m Ellie Honan, a university teacher in ecology and marine biology at the University of Liverpool and a PhD researcher in seabird movement ecology and diet with Durham University and the British Antarctic Survey.

title: Hello!
text: This webpage was put together for students of ENVS255 to act as a resource for their individual projects, but will hopefully be useful for anyone starting to work with QGIS and R. The purpose of this platform isn’t to provide instruction on how to use the datasets, but instead to make you aware of what kinds of data is available. The lists are non-exhaustive!

title: Tracking Data
text: For the purpose of your assignment, you will be using tracking data collected and processed by other scientists. If you wanted to publish any work using this data, you would first have to seek permission from those who own the original data. In the assignment, please only use data that is free to download in the first place.

title: Tracking Data
text:
Where to access tracking data
You may already have come across a study that interests you and are interested in using the same data as in the paper. In this case, most recent peer-reviewed publications will have a section (scroll down to before the references) noting where the data for the paper is stored, and whether it is open access.
Some common repositories you may see referenced are Zenodo, Dryad and figshare. Sometimes the original data can be found in the paper’s supplementary information, but not always.
If you have not decided on a specific study, you can look for available data on specific animal tracking repositories.
The website wildlife data.org has a good, but not exhaustive list of repositories that include tracking data.



MOVEBANK is an excellent resource and good platform for exploring tracking data.



List of repositories

MOVEBANK is a large repository of animal tracking data, and many of these are downloadable from point of access.
Systema Naturae is another large repository - I’ve found that searching by tag type (e.g. “GPS”) in the datasets search bar lends best results.
The MOTUS database, provided by Birds Canada, hosts data collected by the Motus Wildlife Tracking System, which we discussed in the earlier practicals.
Zoatrack is primarly focused on Australia, but is an excellent resource (this is a cached version on the Internet Archive’s Wayback Machine as Zoatrack went offline in 2024).


Species specific repositories:
Turtles: seaturtle.org
Seabirds: The Seabird Tracking Database hosts tracking data on, you guessed it, seabirds!

title: Mapping the Poles
text:
Quantarctica, managed by the Norsk Polar Institut, is a collection of Antarctic geographical datasets for research, education, operations, and management in Antarctica. It integrates with QGIS, but the data in the package can also be used in other software.
The SCAR Antarctic Digital Database hosts extensive topographic (coastlines, rock outcrop, contours, etc) data sets on Antarctica.
The Australian Antarctic Data Centre hosts Australia’s Antarctic Data.
For bathymetric (depth) data in Antarctica, IBSCO, the International Bathymetric Chart of the Southern Ocean is the authoritative map.



QGreenland is similar to Quantarctica but for Greenland.

title: Mapping the Poles
text:

The SCAR Antarctic Digital Database hosts extensive topographic (coastlines, rock outcrop, contours, etc) data sets on Antarctica.
The Australian Antarctic Data Centre hosts Australia’s Antarctic Data.
For bathymetric (depth) data in Antarctica, IBSCO, the International Bathymetric Chart of the Southern Ocean is the authoritative map.

The purpose of this platform isn’t to provide instruction on how to use the datasets listed here, but instead to make you aware of what kinds of data is available. The lists are non-exhaustive!

title: Basemaps
text:
Here you’ll find links to landmass outlines of varying degrees of specificity. These are usually supplied as shapefiles. When you’re choosing your basemap, think about what resolution you require, and how this balances with processing time. For example, a global map of humpack whale migration will not require every inch of the coastline to be fully rendered, but if you were looking at say penguin movements in a small bay in New Zealand, you would want a higher accuracy basemap.
General:
If you’re using QGIS, one of the easiest ways to add a simple basemap is to use the XYZ Tiles option within the software itself - with this you can add Open Street Maps and Google Satellite Maps.
Global:

Natural Earth Data - this is a great resource, and has a R package.
Frequently updated Geopolitical boundaries
Shapefiles for individual countries

UK:

Digimap

Antarctica:

The SCAR Antarctic Digital Database keeps a frequently updates high resolution shapefile of the Antarctic coastline.

title: Environmental Data
text:
In our practical sessions, we’ve used various types of environmental data. These data have either been static or dynamic.
Static environmental data layers are those that are (relatively) fixed - think water depth or outlines of countries. In reality, the whims of plate tectonics and geopolitics mean there are no truly ‘fixed’ data layers, but for the purpose of making maps or conducting analyses we consider them as permanent.
In contrast, dynamic environmental layers are those that vary temporally. For example, the temperature of the ocean’s surface in a given area will vary across different timescales - usually with small differences between days, but greater differences between weeks or months.
In practice, this means that if you wanted to see if animals used different water depths over the course of a breeding season, you could use the same file for depth for everyday of the breeding season. However, if you wanted to see if they used warmer or colder waters throughout the breeding season, you would need a different file of sea surface temperature for everyday of your study period.

title: Environmental Data
text:
In our practical sessions, we’ve used various types of environmental data. These data have either been static or dynamic.
Static environmental data layers are those that are (relatively) fixed - think water depth or outlines of countries. In reality, the whims of plate tectonics and geopolitics mean there are no truly ‘fixed’ data layers, but for the purpose of making maps or conducting analyses we consider them as permanent.
In contrast, dynamic environmental layers are those that vary temporally. For example, the temperature of the ocean’s surface in a given area will vary across different timescales - usually with small differences between days, but greater differences between weeks or months.
In practice, this means that if you wanted to see if animals used different water depths over the course of a breeding season, you could use the same file for depth for everyday of the breeding season. However, if you wanted to see if they used warmer or colder waters throughout the breeding season, you would need a different file of sea surface temperature for everyday of your study period.

title: Environmental Data
text:
Choosing scale
What data product you use will vary based on the resolution of data required to answer your question. If you’re curious of how close to the sea-ice edge Emperor penguins forage in Antarctica, you might use a relatively course sea-ice edge product - one of the most frequently used is at a scale of 25km2, as the penguins’ foraging trips can span 100s of kilometers. You would also want a product with a near-daily resolution. However, if you wanted to see how a mangrove boundary changed over the course of a year in a small inlet in Indonesia, you would want a much higher resolution image, potentially sub-meter in scale, and monthly imagery would suffice.
Here, I’ve listed some portals for where you can access environmental data. They are all free to access, but most will require you to make an account. I haven’t separated them out by the type of data, as many of the portals host multiple types of environmental data, encompassing both oceans and land.

title: Software
text: In the practicals and on this website we will be using only software which is free and open-source, meaning it is available for anyone to download and use free of charge.

title: Software
text:
QGIS
In the practicals for ENVS255 we have been using QGIS, which is available to download at here. QGIS is a free and open-source Geographic Information System (GIS) application designed for spatial data visualisation, analysis, and management. It is widely used in research and industry for creating, editing, and analysing geospatial data.
Alongside what we worked on in the taught practicals, there is a plethora of excellent resources for learning how to best use QGIS available online - some of these are listed in the Tutorials tab of this website.
